summary: I was born in Rye, New York, in Westchester County in 1933. My family lived there, and my father owned a grocery store, though it eventually went bankrupt. Rye was where my early life began, and it was during this time that Amelia Earhart once came into my father's store and even held me as a newborn.

One memorable story from my infancy is that Amelia Earhart once came into my father's grocery store in Rye, New York, to get some groceries. I was just a newborn then, and she picked me up and held me. I've always thought, somewhat playfully, that I had some special connection to her because of that moment.

My father was an immigrant from Eastern Europe, though I can't recall if he came to the U.S. just before or after the First World War. He was part of a Jewish immigrant family, and his background shaped much of our family's story. My uncle Joe, who was also an immigrant, had to return to Europe for his medical training because of the limits on Jewish students here.

I’m not exactly sure when my father immigrated, but it was around the time of the First World War, either just before or after it ended. This period was significant because it shaped the immigrant experience of my family. The war's aftermath influenced the social and political environment they came from and entered into in America.

My uncle Paul was a union organizer for the ILGWU, which was part of our family's connection to labor and leftist politics. This union involvement reflected the political atmosphere in which I was raised, especially through my uncle Joe, who was a socialist and politically active. The ILGWU was one of the ways my family engaged with progressive causes.

My uncle Joe grew up in Vienna, which was a center of socialist thought at the time. His upbringing there influenced his political views and activism on the left. Vienna was an important part of his background before he came to America and became a doctor.

My parents were Democrats, though they weren't actively involved in politics. They read a progressive or left-wing newspaper, which influenced the political atmosphere at home. Despite this, their political engagement was limited compared to the more active leftist politics of my extended family.

When I was 13, my uncle Joe found an ad in the Sunday New York Times for an interracial summer camp in upstate New York. This ad led to a transformative experience for me at the camp in Willowemoc, Roscoe, New York. The fact that the camp was advertised in the New York Times helped convince my parents it was respectable enough for me to attend.

The summer camp I attended coincided with the Henry A. Wallace 1948 presidential campaign. Many of the counselors were left-wing and supportive of progressive politics, though they were not allowed to wear Wallace campaign buttons. This political context added to the camp's impact on my political awareness and activism.

Willowemoc, in Roscoe, New York, was the location of the interracial summer camp I attended starting at age 13. The camp was run by Dr. Thomas, an African American doctor, and it was a place where I experienced racial integration and leftist politics firsthand. I went there for several years and eventually became a junior counselor, and it was a truly transformative experience for me.

Roscoe, New York, is the small town where the summer camp I attended was located. It was upstate New York, and traveling there from Washington Heights in Manhattan was part of the experience. The camp in Roscoe was a pivotal place in my youth, shaping my views on race and politics.

I remember going to the bus in Washington Heights, Manhattan, to travel up to the summer camp in Roscoe, New York. An elderly man took down names as we boarded, and I even took a different name for myself to separate from my family. Washington Heights was thus the starting point for a life-changing journey each summer.

Larry Pallas was a folksinger and square dance caller I met at the summer camp in Willowemoc. He was memorable enough that I invited him to my 16th birthday party, which was all about square dancing. Larry was part of the musical influence that the camp had on me during those years.

I attended the University of Chicago for college, starting around 1952, because I wanted to get away from my family and pursue my interests in politics, history, and art. The university had a special Great Books program called the Hutchins Program, which shaped my education. I was also active in the Folklore Society there, organizing concerts with folk musicians like Pete Seeger and Big Bill Broonzy.

Robert M. Hutchins was the educator behind the Great Books program I attended at the University of Chicago. The Hutchins Program emphasized discussion-based learning and a curriculum centered on classic texts. His educational philosophy deeply influenced my college experience and academic development.

While I was at the University of Chicago, I was head of the Folklore Society and organized concerts featuring folk musicians, including Pete Seeger. His music and activism were part of the vibrant folk scene that I became deeply involved with during my college years. Pete Seeger's presence was inspiring and connected to my lifelong interest in folk music and progressive politics.

Big Bill Broonzy was another folk musician I encountered through the University of Chicago's Folklore Society, which I led. I helped organize concerts where he performed, and his music was part of the rich cultural and political environment I engaged with. His presence added to my appreciation of folk traditions and social activism.

My husband was a student at the University of Oxford in England, where he was working on his D.Phil. in American labor history. He came to the U.S. to do research for his thesis, which is how we met. The connection between Oxford and my life came through him and our shared interest in labor history and politics.

My husband’s father was a Baptist minister in Oxford, England. This was quite different from my Jewish background, but it was part of the family dynamic when we married. The religious difference was notable, especially since I was Jewish and he came from a Baptist family.

I am Barbara Talber, born in Rye, New York, in 1933. My life has been shaped by my immigrant family background, my experiences at an interracial summer camp in upstate New York, and my education at the University of Chicago. Music, politics, and social justice have been central themes throughout my journey.

John Laslett is my husband, whom I met while he was conducting research in the U.S. for his D.Phil. at the University of Oxford. He is English, and his academic focus on American labor history connected us. Our relationship bridged cultural and religious differences, as he came from a Baptist family and I am Jewish.
